turns.user_message: Look, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not looking to quit smoking. It's just something I do. I've been doing it for 20 years, and I'm not hurting anyone.
step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
[Session Phase: 2 — Exploration]

Therapist:
step7_response: I sense some resistance, but I'm here to listen and support you in any way I can. Can you tell me a little more about what you mean by "it's just something I do"?

turns.user_message: It's just something I do when I'm stressed or bored. And, you know, all my friends at work smoke, so it's just part of the culture. I don't see it as a big deal. Plus, I've tried quitting before, and it didn't work out. I don't want to waste my time or money on something that's not gonna stick.
step2_rag_chunks: Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.
Keeping your hands busy with activities such as drawing, writing, or using a stress ball can help reduce the urge to smoke.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the MOTIVATION phase. You understand their triggers. Now explore their motivation: why do they want to quit — is it health, family, cost, doctor advice? Have they tried before and what happened? Be empathetic. Do NOT push strategies yet.

[Session Phase: 3 — Motivation]

You are in the MOTIVATION phase. You understand their triggers. Now explore their motivation: why do they want to quit — is it health, family, cost, doctor advice? Have they tried before and what happened? Be empathetic. Do NOT push strategies yet.
[Patient Profile]
- Smoking status: cigarettes for 20 years
- Known triggers: stress, boredom
- Tried strategies: quitting before → did not work out
[Clinical Knowledge]
- Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.
- Keeping your hands busy with activities such as drawing, writing, or using a stress ball can help reduce the urge to smoke.
- Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
[Recent Conversation]
Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?
Patient: Look, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not looking to quit smoking. It's just something I do. I've been doing it for 20 years, and I'm not hurting anyone.

Therapist: I understand that you might not be considering quitting right now, Jamie. But I want to acknowledge that you're here, and that's a step in the right direction. Can you tell me a little bit about what smoking means to you? How does it fit into your daily life?

Patient: It's just something I do when I'm stressed or bored. And, you know, all my friends at work smoke, so it's just part of the culture. I don't see it as a big deal. Plus, I've tried quitting before, and it didn't work out. I don't want to waste my time or money on something that's not gonna stick.
